label: Tech Minimal
description: Developer blog variant with cleaner, faster, docs-adjacent presentation.
signatureMotif: clean technical hierarchy, docs-adjacent rhythm, and restrained developer polish
promptHints: Keep the reading flow technical and efficient rather than lifestyle-editorial.
Use code-friendly surfaces and documentation-adjacent hierarchy where it helps.

layouts: Use a narrow reading column with a sticky right-side table of contents and slim metadata rail for long technical posts.
Compose the homepage as a compact article feed with featured post first, then dense title-and-excerpt rows separated by hairline dividers.
Build post headers with left-aligned title, small monospace metadata line, and optional tag cluster above a restrained hero-free intro.
Use docs-adjacent content pages with a 240px left sidebar for categories and a clean main pane for articles or API notes.
Present archive and tag pages as two-column lists with dates in a fixed narrow column and titles plus summaries in the wider column.
motifs: Pair sans headings with monospace body accents, using code-block surfaces, muted borders, and a cool neutral palette with one blue accent.
Use 1px dividers, 0.7rem corner radius, and low-contrast cards so structure feels engineered rather than decorative.
Let whitespace and typography carry hierarchy, with compact section spacing, precise line lengths, and understated interactive states.
Treat code snippets, callouts, and metadata as first-class elements using documentation-like panels instead of magazine-style visual flourishes.
antiPatterns: Avoid oversized lifestyle hero imagery or full-bleed editorial photography that interrupts the technical reading rhythm.
Never use loud gradient-heavy cards, glassmorphism, or neon glows that make the blog feel like a startup marketing page.
Avoid center-aligned landing-page compositions for article pages; this variant should read like documentation, not a personal brand splash.
Do not over-round surfaces or stack chunky shadows, because soft playful UI undermines the precise engineering tone.
